---
title: "High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6"
author: "Rich Herrington <br> DRI, Research Computing Services"
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
---

# High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6

## Overview

This course introduces high-performance computing (HPC) using the Lonestar6 system at the Texas Advanced Computing Center (TACC).

The course is designed as both:

- a textbook
- a lab manual
- a practical workflow guide

We move from:

- basic system access
- to Linux fundamentals
- to SLURM job scheduling
- to compiling software
- to parallel computing
- to containers
- to a capstone project
- to a practical appendix

## Learning Objectives

By the end of the course, students should be able to:

- explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes
- navigate Linux confidently on a shared cluster
- manage files, permissions, and environment variables
- load software through modules
- submit and monitor SLURM batch jobs
- debug basic job failures
- compile C and C++ software for HPC systems
- understand the role of MPI, OpenMP, and containers in modern HPC workflows

## Course Design

Each chapter includes:

- overview
- mental model
- commands
- walkthrough
- common mistakes
- lab
- stretch goal

## Suggested Use

This material works well for:

- upper-level undergraduate instruction
- graduate onboarding
- research-computing workshops
- self-paced training for new cluster users
